display_name: Cape Horn Passage — Around South America to California

segments.from: New York, Boston, or other East Coast port
segments.to: Cape Horn, southern tip of South America (Tierra del Fuego)
segments.travel_time: 2-4 months depending on vessel and weather
segments.cost: $150-$300 cabin class; $75-$150 steerage. Many Forty-Niners formed joint stock companies — groups of men who pooled money to charter a vessel and split costs at $300-$500 per share.
segments.conditions: The first leg took ships south through the Atlantic, crossing the equator in the doldrums — days of dead calm, blistering heat, and dwindling fresh water. Passengers on chartered vessels quickly discovered that ships purchased cheaply for the voyage were often rotting hulks barely fit for the harbor, let alone the open ocean. Scurvy appeared after two months when fresh provisions ran out. The monotony was crushing — day after day of empty horizon, the creak of rigging, the slap of waves against the hull.

segments.distance: approximately 1,000 miles from the Horn to Valparaiso
segments.travel_time: 2-6 weeks to round the Horn itself; ships sometimes beat against westerly gales for weeks without making progress
segments.cost: included in passage; resupply at Valparaiso at inflated prices
segments.conditions: Rounding Cape Horn was the most dangerous passage in commercial sailing. The Southern Ocean funneled between Antarctica and South America, driving waves fifty feet high through Drake Passage. Westerly gales blew without cease. Ships were coated in ice. Sails shredded. Men went aloft in freezing spray to reef canvas on yards that pitched sixty degrees in each direction. The temperature dropped below freezing and stayed there for weeks. Some vessels were driven back east around the Horn three or four times before finally clawing through to the Pacific.

segments.from: Valparaiso, Chile (or direct from Cape Horn)
segments.travel_time: 6-10 weeks from Valparaiso; total voyage 5-8 months. Clipper ships in the 1850s made the entire New York-to-San Francisco run in 89-100 days.
segments.cost: included in original passage. Clipper ship passages commanded premium fares: $200-$500 cabin.
segments.conditions: The Pacific leg was calmer but interminable — thousands of miles of open water, the coast of South America and then Central America sliding past to port. Ships sometimes stopped at Pacific ports for fresh water and provisions. Arriving in San Francisco, passengers found a harbor choked with abandoned vessels — crews had deserted for the gold fields, and hundreds of ships rotted at anchor or were hauled ashore and converted into warehouses and hotels.

who_used_it: Gold Rush emigrants from the East Coast, particularly from New England port cities (Boston, New Bedford, Salem, Nantucket) with maritime traditions. Merchants who needed to ship heavy cargo — mining equipment, lumber, manufactured goods — that could not survive the overland trail or the Panama crossing. Entire families who preferred the relative safety of remaining aboard ship for the full voyage rather than risking the overland trail. An estimated 40,000-50,000 people sailed around Cape Horn to California between 1849 and 1860.
narrative_hook: In {year}, {ancestor_name} sailed from {origin} the long way — 15,000 miles around Cape Horn, through the worst seas on earth, five to eight months of open ocean — to reach {destination} with belongings intact and a body battered by scurvy, storms, and the endless patience required to sail halfway around the world for gold.
source_references: Oscar Lewis, 'Sea Routes to the Gold Fields: The Migration by Water to California in 1849-1852' (Alfred A. Knopf, 1949)
James P. Delgado, 'To California by Sea: A Maritime History of the California Gold Rush' (University of South Carolina Press, 1990)
Raymond A. Rydell, 'Cape Horn to the Pacific: The Rise and Decline of an Ocean Highway' (University of California Press, 1952)
John Haskell Kemble, 'The Panama Route, 1848-1869' (University of California Press, 1943)
